title: TARDIVE DYSKINESIA Recognized at a Routine Community-Mental-Health Visit in a 63-Year-Old Woman with Long-Standing Schizophrenia on ~20 Years of a First-Generation Antipsychotic (Haloperidol) Plus Type 2 Diabetes — New, Persistent, INVOLUNTARY Orofacial-Buccolingual Movements (Lip Smacking, Puckering, Tongue Protrusion/Writhing, Facial Grimacing) and Choreoathetoid Finger Movements She Is Largely UNAWARE Of: DISTINGUISHING the Four Antipsychotic Movement Syndromes by TIMING (ACUTE DYSTONIA in Hours–Days, AKATHISIA in Days–Weeks, Drug-Induced PSEUDOPARKINSONISM in Weeks–Months, and TARDIVE DYSKINESIA After MONTHS–YEARS of Dopamine Blockade — Often PERSISTENT/Irreversible), QUANTIFYING with the AIMS (Abnormal Involuntary Movement Scale) and Ruling Out Mimics, NOTIFYING Psychiatry to Re-Evaluate/Minimize or SWITCH the Antipsychotic (NEVER an Abrupt Stop — Withdrawal-Emergent Dyskinesia + Relapse) and Start an FDA-Approved VMAT2 INHIBITOR (VALBENAZINE or DEUTETRABENAZINE) with QTc/Somnolence/Mood Monitoring, the Classic Test Point that ANTICHOLINERGICS (Benztropine/Diphenhydramine) WORSEN TD (They Treat Dystonia/Pseudoparkinsonism, NOT TD), and Swallowing/Safety + Non-Judgmental Psychosocial Support for a Stigmatizing, Quality-of-Life-Limiting Condition
subtitle: TARDIVE DYSKINESIA (TD) is a LATE ('tardive' = delayed) movement disorder caused by MONTHS-TO-YEARS of dopamine D2-receptor blockade (chronic antipsychotics — especially FIRST-GENERATION agents like haloperidol, but also second-generation agents and dopamine-blocking antiemetics such as metoclopramide/prochlorperazine), thought to arise from D2-receptor up-regulation/supersensitivity; it is frequently PERSISTENT and may be IRREVERSIBLE, so early recognition matters. This case follows B. Aldridge, a 63-year-old woman with long-standing schizophrenia maintained on haloperidol for about 20 years (with type 2 diabetes — older age, female sex, diabetes, mood disorder, and prior extrapyramidal symptoms all raise TD risk), seen for a routine long-acting injection when the nurse notices NEW, repetitive, INVOLUNTARY OROFACIAL-BUCCOLINGUAL-MASTICATORY movements (lip smacking and puckering, tongue protrusion/writhing, chewing, facial grimacing) plus slow CHOREOATHETOID finger/hand movements — present at rest, worse with distraction, absent in sleep, and movements the patient is largely UNAWARE of. The nurse must DISTINGUISH the four antipsychotic-associated movement syndromes by their TIMING and features: ACUTE DYSTONIA (hours–days; sustained spasms — torticollis, oculogyric crisis, laryngospasm = airway emergency) responds to an ANTICHOLINERGIC (benztropine) or diphenhydramine; AKATHISIA (days–weeks; subjective inner restlessness/inability to sit still) responds to dose reduction, a beta-blocker (propranolol), or a benzodiazepine; drug-induced PSEUDOPARKINSONISM (weeks–months; bradykinesia, cogwheel rigidity, resting tremor, masked facies) responds to dose reduction/switch or an anticholinergic; and TARDIVE DYSKINESIA (months–years; the picture here) is treated very differently. The nurse QUANTIFIES the movements with the AIMS (Abnormal Involuntary Movement Scale) — the standardized tool done at antipsychotic initiation and then periodically (more often with high-risk/first-generation agents) — and helps rule out mimics (Huntington disease, primary dystonias, stereotypies, ill-fitting dentures, metoclopramide exposure, withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia). MANAGEMENT: NOTIFY psychiatry to re-evaluate the ongoing need for and DOSE of the antipsychotic and consider SWITCHING to a lower-TD-risk agent — but NEVER abruptly STOP it (abrupt withdrawal can cause withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia and psychotic relapse; changes are tapered/adjusted by the prescriber) — and START an FDA-approved VMAT2 INHIBITOR (VALBENAZINE [Ingrezza] or DEUTETRABENAZINE [Austedo]), which reduce presynaptic dopamine release and are first-line for TD, monitoring for somnolence, QTc prolongation, parkinsonism, and mood/suicidality. A CLASSIC NCLEX TEST POINT: ANTICHOLINERGICS (benztropine, diphenhydramine, trihexyphenidyl) and simply INCREASING the antipsychotic do NOT help TD and can WORSEN or merely MASK it — anticholinergics are for acute dystonia/pseudoparkinsonism, not TD. The nurse also assesses SWALLOWING/aspiration and gait safety as movements can impair function, and provides NON-JUDGMENTAL psychosocial support for a stigmatizing, distressing, quality-of-life-limiting condition while reinforcing adherence and follow-up. Step-6 evaluates whether each later finding reflects the patient IMPROVING, WORSENING, or remaining UNCHANGED.

patient.name: B. Aldridge
patient.admitDate: Established patient at the community mental-health clinic with schizophrenia maintained on haloperidol for ~20 years (currently a long-acting injectable), plus type 2 diabetes. Seen today for her routine LAI visit. Over the past few months staff and her sister have noticed new mouth and tongue movements; today the nurse observes persistent involuntary orofacial movements and finger movements that the patient is largely unaware of and that are not present when she sleeps.
patient.room: Community Mental-Health Clinic, Med Room / Exam Room 1; sister present

vitals.spo2: 98% on room air; T 36.8°C; alert, oriented, psychiatrically stable (no acute psychosis); persistent involuntary LIP smacking/puckering, TONGUE protrusion/writhing, and facial grimacing plus slow choreoathetoid finger movements — present at rest, increase with distraction, and the patient is largely unaware of them

98% RA; T 36.8°C; AIMS completed = moderate orofacial + mild extremity dyskinesia; NO sustained spasm/torticollis/oculogyric crisis (not acute dystonia), NO inner restlessness/pacing (not akathisia), NO bradykinesia/cogwheel rigidity/resting tremor/masked facies (not pseudoparkinsonism); movements absent in sleep; swallow screen intact today

vitals.time: 6-week follow-up — VMAT2 inhibitor started, antipsychotic optimized
vitals.spo2: 99% RA; T 36.7°C; psychiatry minimized the antipsychotic dose (no abrupt stop) and started valbenazine; orofacial movements noticeably less frequent, eating more comfortably; no excess sedation, no new parkinsonism, tolerating the medication
99% RA; T 36.8°C; AIMS score substantially lower, movements much reduced, no psychotic relapse on the optimized regimen, QTc stable on monitoring; engaged and less self-conscious in group

vitals.time: ALTERNATE course — anticholinergic given + abrupt antipsychotic stop (WRONG)
vitals.spo2: 97% RA; T 37.0°C; after benztropine was added for the 'tremor' and the antipsychotic was abruptly discontinued, the orofacial dyskinesia is WORSE and she has emerging psychotic symptoms (withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia + relapse) — illustrating the two classic errors

vitals.time: Recovery — 6-month follow-up on the correct regimen
vitals.spo2: 99% RA; T 36.8°C; TD markedly improved on the VMAT2 inhibitor with a minimized lower-risk antipsychotic, psychiatrically stable, swallowing/gait safe, engaged in care with ongoing AIMS monitoring

labs.n: PRESENTATION + WHY tardive dyskinesia happens (late, from chronic dopamine blockade)
labs.v: 63 y/o woman, ~20 years on haloperidol (a first-generation antipsychotic) plus type 2 diabetes, with NEW persistent INVOLUNTARY orofacial-buccolingual-masticatory movements (lip smacking/puckering, tongue protrusion/writhing, chewing, facial grimacing) and choreoathetoid finger movements — present at rest, worse with distraction, absent in sleep, and she is largely UNAWARE of them. KEY PRINCIPLE: TARDIVE DYSKINESIA is a LATE complication ('tardive' = delayed) of MONTHS-TO-YEARS of dopamine D2-receptor BLOCKADE, attributed to D2-receptor up-regulation/supersensitivity; it is often PERSISTENT and may be IRREVERSIBLE. RISK FACTORS: longer cumulative dose/DURATION of dopamine-blocking drugs (first-generation antipsychotics highest, but also second-generation agents and antiemetics like METOCLOPRAMIDE/prochlorperazine), OLDER age, FEMALE sex, DIABETES, mood disorder, and prior extrapyramidal symptoms.
labs.r: TD = LATE (months–years) involuntary movements from chronic D2 blockade → receptor supersensitivity; often persistent/irreversible. Classic = orofacial-buccolingual (lip smacking, tongue protrusion, grimacing) + choreoathetoid limbs. Risk: long duration of antipsychotics (first-gen highest; also metoclopramide), older age, female, diabetes, mood disorder, prior EPS.

labs.n: DISTINGUISHING the FOUR antipsychotic movement syndromes by TIMING
labs.v: ACUTE DYSTONIA: onset HOURS–DAYS; sustained, painful muscle contractions — torticollis, oculogyric crisis, trismus, and LARYNGOSPASM (an airway EMERGENCY) → treat with an ANTICHOLINERGIC (benztropine) or DIPHENHYDRAMINE IM/IV. AKATHISIA: DAYS–WEEKS; subjective INNER RESTLESSNESS with inability to sit still, pacing, fidgeting → reduce dose, a BETA-BLOCKER (propranolol), or a benzodiazepine. Drug-induced PSEUDOPARKINSONISM: WEEKS–MONTHS; BRADYKINESIA, cogwheel RIGIDITY, resting TREMOR, masked facies, shuffling gait → reduce dose/switch agent or add an ANTICHOLINERGIC. TARDIVE DYSKINESIA: MONTHS–YEARS; repetitive, involuntary CHOREOATHETOID movements, classically OROFACIAL — managed entirely differently (VMAT2 inhibitor; anticholinergics WORSEN it). Recognizing the TIME COURSE is what separates a benztropine-responsive syndrome from TD.
labs.r: By TIMING: ACUTE DYSTONIA (hrs–days; spasms/oculogyric/laryngospasm → anticholinergic/diphenhydramine); AKATHISIA (days–wks; inner restlessness → dose↓/propranolol/benzo); PSEUDOPARKINSONISM (wks–mos; bradykinesia/rigidity/tremor → dose↓/switch/anticholinergic); TARDIVE DYSKINESIA (mos–yrs; orofacial choreoathetoid → VMAT2 inhibitor; anticholinergics WORSEN).

labs.n: AIMS (Abnormal Involuntary Movement Scale) + screening schedule + ruling out mimics
labs.v: The AIMS is the standardized 12-item exam used to detect and QUANTIFY abnormal movements: facial/oral (muscles of facial expression, lips/perioral, jaw, tongue), extremities (upper and lower), trunk, plus global severity, incapacitation, and the patient's AWARENESS/distress, with items on dental/denture status. Best practice: perform AIMS at antipsychotic INITIATION (baseline) and then PERIODICALLY (e.g., every 6–12 months — MORE often with high-risk patients or first-generation agents) and whenever new movements appear, so emerging TD is caught early when intervention is most useful. RULE OUT MIMICS before attributing to TD: Huntington disease and other primary movement disorders, idiopathic dystonias, stereotypies, ill-fitting dentures/edentulous mouthing, dopamine-blocking ANTIEMETIC exposure (metoclopramide), and WITHDRAWAL-EMERGENT dyskinesia after a recent antipsychotic reduction/stop.
labs.r: AIMS = standardized 12-item scale (face/oral, extremities, trunk + severity/incapacitation/awareness) to detect + quantify movements; do at BASELINE then periodically (q6–12 mo, more often if high-risk/first-gen) and when new movements appear. Rule out mimics: Huntington, primary dystonia, stereotypies, dentures, metoclopramide, withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia.

labs.n: MANAGEMENT — re-evaluate/minimize or SWITCH the antipsychotic (NEVER abrupt stop) + VMAT2 INHIBITORS
labs.v: Steps once TD is identified and quantified: (1) NOTIFY the prescriber/psychiatry and re-evaluate the ongoing NEED and DOSE of the antipsychotic — use the LOWEST effective dose and consider SWITCHING to a lower-TD-risk agent (e.g., a second-generation agent; clozapine has the lowest risk). (2) Do NOT abruptly STOP the antipsychotic — abrupt withdrawal can cause WITHDRAWAL-EMERGENT dyskinesia and PSYCHOTIC RELAPSE; any taper/change is made by the prescriber while monitoring mental status. (3) START an FDA-approved VMAT2 INHIBITOR — VALBENAZINE (Ingrezza, once daily) or DEUTETRABENAZINE (Austedo) — which deplete/reduce presynaptic dopamine release and are FIRST-LINE, evidence-based treatment for TD; monitor for SOMNOLENCE, QTc PROLONGATION, parkinsonism, and DEPRESSION/suicidality (a particular concern with older tetrabenazine, classically used in Huntington chorea). Reassess response with serial AIMS scores.
labs.r: Manage TD: notify psychiatry → minimize dose / SWITCH to a lower-risk antipsychotic (clozapine lowest), but NEVER abruptly stop (withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia + relapse). START a VMAT2 INHIBITOR (valbenazine or deutetrabenazine) = first-line; monitor somnolence, QTc, parkinsonism, mood/suicidality. Track response with serial AIMS.

labs.n: ANTICHOLINERGICS WORSEN TD + swallowing/safety + psychosocial support + PERTINENT NEGATIVES
labs.v: CLASSIC TEST POINT: ANTICHOLINERGICS (benztropine, trihexyphenidyl, diphenhydramine) treat acute DYSTONIA and PSEUDOPARKINSONISM but do NOT help TD and can WORSEN it — they should be reviewed and often REDUCED/STOPPED in TD. Likewise, simply INCREASING the antipsychotic may transiently MASK movements but worsens the underlying disorder and is not the answer. The nurse assesses FUNCTION/SAFETY: SWALLOWING (orofacial/tongue movements can impair chewing/swallowing → aspiration risk and weight loss) and gait/dexterity, and provides NON-JUDGMENTAL psychosocial support because TD is visible, STIGMATIZING, and distressing and can harm self-esteem and treatment adherence. Educate on the VMAT2 medication and the importance of NOT stopping the antipsychotic on her own, and maintain regular AIMS follow-up. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice — these are WRONG): do NOT give benztropine/diphenhydramine for TD; do NOT abruptly stop the antipsychotic; do NOT increase the antipsychotic to suppress the movements; do NOT dismiss the movements as a 'habit' or 'just old age'; and do NOT skip AIMS screening on patients receiving antipsychotics.
labs.r: ANTICHOLINERGICS (benztropine/diphenhydramine) WORSEN TD (they're for dystonia/pseudoparkinsonism) → review/stop them; don't just ↑ the antipsychotic (masks, worsens). Assess SWALLOWING/aspiration + gait safety; give NON-JUDGMENTAL support (stigmatizing/distressing). NEGATIVES: don't give anticholinergics, don't abrupt-stop the antipsychotic, don't ↑ it to suppress movements, don't dismiss as habit/aging, don't skip AIMS.

labs.v: 14 (moderate-to-severe — orofacial items prominent with mild extremity involvement; used as a baseline to track response to the VMAT2 inhibitor)
7.8% (elevated — her type 2 diabetes is both a comorbidity to manage and a recognized risk factor for tardive dyskinesia)
438 ms (within normal limits — checked before/while starting a VMAT2 inhibitor, which can prolong the QT; monitor with other QT-prolonging drugs)
152 mg/dL (elevated — consistent with her type 2 diabetes; relevant to overall metabolic monitoring on antipsychotics)

labs.n: TSH (screening to exclude a contributing/mimicking cause)
labs.v: 2.1 mIU/L (normal — thyroid dysfunction and other secondary causes of abnormal movements are reasonably excluded; supports a drug-induced tardive dyskinesia diagnosis)

nursesNote: Community mental-health clinic — routine LAI visit. 63 y/o woman, schizophrenia ~20 years on haloperidol, plus type 2 diabetes. Observed NEW persistent involuntary orofacial movements (lip smacking/puckering, tongue protrusion/writhing, facial grimacing) and slow choreoathetoid finger movements — present at rest, worse with distraction, absent in sleep, and she is largely unaware of them; her sister reports they began a few months ago. Recognized this as TARDIVE DYSKINESIA, a late complication of chronic dopamine blockade — NOT acute dystonia (no sustained spasm/oculogyric crisis), NOT akathisia (no inner restlessness/pacing), and NOT pseudoparkinsonism (no bradykinesia/cogwheel rigidity/resting tremor/masked facies). Completed an AIMS (score 14, moderate-to-severe, orofacial predominant) for a baseline and to track response; screened swallowing (intact today) and gait safety. Helped rule out mimics (no metoclopramide use, no recent antipsychotic stop to suggest withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia, TSH normal). Notified psychiatry: plan to minimize the antipsychotic dose and consider switching to a lower-TD-risk agent WITHOUT abruptly stopping it (to avoid withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia and relapse) and to start a VMAT2 inhibitor (valbenazine) with QTc/somnolence/mood monitoring (baseline QTc 438 ms). Did NOT give benztropine or diphenhydramine (anticholinergics worsen TD), did NOT increase the antipsychotic to suppress the movements, and did NOT dismiss the movements as a habit or aging. Provided non-judgmental support regarding a visible, stigmatizing condition, taught the VMAT2 medication and the importance of not stopping the antipsychotic on her own, and arranged ongoing AIMS follow-up. Sister included with the patient's consent.
providerOrders: DIAGNOSIS/ASSESSMENT: tardive dyskinesia attributed to long-term antipsychotic (dopamine D2-blockade) therapy; perform and document an AIMS for baseline severity and to track response

RULE OUT MIMICS: review for dopamine-blocking antiemetic use (metoclopramide/prochlorperazine), recent antipsychotic reduction/stop (withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia), primary movement disorders, and ill-fitting dentures; TSH and glucose as indicated

ANTIPSYCHOTIC RE-EVALUATION: psychiatry to use the lowest effective dose and consider switching to a lower-TD-risk agent (e.g., a second-generation antipsychotic; clozapine lowest risk) — do NOT abruptly discontinue (risk of withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia and psychotic relapse)
VMAT2 INHIBITOR: start valbenazine (or deutetrabenazine) as first-line treatment for tardive dyskinesia; monitor for somnolence, QTc prolongation, parkinsonism, and depression/suicidality

AVOID ANTICHOLINERGICS: do NOT use benztropine, trihexyphenidyl, or diphenhydramine for tardive dyskinesia (these worsen it; they are for acute dystonia/pseudoparkinsonism); review and reduce/stop any current anticholinergic
QTc/MONITORING: baseline and periodic ECG QTc while on a VMAT2 inhibitor; review concurrent QT-prolonging medications
FUNCTION/SAFETY: assess swallowing/aspiration risk and gait/dexterity; dietitian/speech referral if swallowing is affected; monitor weight
DIABETES/METABOLIC: continue diabetes management and routine metabolic monitoring on antipsychotic therapy (A1c, glucose, lipids, weight)

EDUCATION/SUPPORT: provide non-judgmental psychosocial support for a stigmatizing condition, teach the VMAT2 medication, reinforce NOT stopping the antipsychotic independently, and maintain regular AIMS follow-up and care coordination

opts.text: Repetitive involuntary lip smacking/puckering, tongue protrusion/writhing, and facial grimacing
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — orofacial-buccolingual-masticatory movements are the classic presentation of tardive dyskinesia.

opts.text: Slow, writhing (choreoathetoid) involuntary finger and hand movements
opts.rat: RELEVANT — extremity choreoathetoid movements are part of the TD picture and are scored on the AIMS.

opts.text: About 20 years of continuous first-generation antipsychotic (haloperidol) therapy
opts.rat: RELEVANT — long cumulative duration of dopamine blockade, especially with a first-generation agent, is the key risk factor.

opts.text: The movements are present at rest, worsen with distraction, and disappear during sleep, and she is largely unaware of them
opts.rat: RELEVANT — this pattern (and limited awareness) is characteristic of TD and helps distinguish it from voluntary or anxious movements.

opts.text: Risk factors of older age, female sex, and type 2 diabetes
opts.rat: RELEVANT — these increase the likelihood of TD and support the diagnosis.

opts.text: Sustained painful neck spasm with the eyes rolling upward (oculogyric crisis)
opts.rat: ⚠️ This describes ACUTE DYSTONIA (hours–days), not TD — she has none of these sustained spasms.

opts.text: Subjective inner restlessness with constant pacing and inability to sit still
opts.rat: ⚠️ This describes AKATHISIA, a different syndrome — she does not report restlessness or pace.

opts.text: This is tardive dyskinesia — a late complication of months-to-years of dopamine blockade that may be persistent or irreversible
opts.rat: CORRECT — the late onset, orofacial choreoathetoid pattern, and chronic antipsychotic exposure define TD.

opts.text: It is distinguished from acute dystonia, akathisia, and pseudoparkinsonism largely by its TIMING (months–years) and movement pattern
opts.rat: CORRECT — recognizing the time course separates TD from the earlier, often anticholinergic-responsive syndromes.

opts.text: An AIMS should be done to quantify severity and track response, and mimics (e.g., Huntington, metoclopramide, withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia) ruled out
opts.rat: CORRECT — standardized assessment and excluding mimics guide diagnosis and monitoring.

opts.text: Management includes notifying psychiatry to minimize/switch the antipsychotic (without abruptly stopping it) and starting a VMAT2 inhibitor
opts.rat: CORRECT — these are the evidence-based steps for TD.

opts.text: Anticholinergics such as benztropine or diphenhydramine would not help and could worsen the movements
opts.rat: CORRECT — anticholinergics treat dystonia/pseudoparkinsonism, not TD, and can aggravate it.

opts.text: Giving benztropine now is the priority treatment for these movements
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Anticholinergics worsen TD; they are indicated for acute dystonia/pseudoparkinsonism, not tardive dyskinesia.

opts.text: Increasing the haloperidol dose is the best way to control the movements
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Raising the antipsychotic may transiently mask TD but worsens the underlying disorder.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Rank these explanations from #1 (MOST likely) to #5 (LEAST).

opts.text: Tardive dyskinesia from long-term antipsychotic (dopamine-blockade) therapy
opts.rat: #1 — MOST likely: late-onset orofacial choreoathetoid movements after ~20 years of antipsychotics are classic TD.

opts.text: Drug-induced pseudoparkinsonism
opts.rat: #2 — On the antipsychotic-EPS differential, but she lacks bradykinesia, cogwheel rigidity, resting tremor, and masked facies.

opts.text: Akathisia
opts.rat: #3 — Also antipsychotic-related, but she has no subjective restlessness or inability to sit still.

opts.text: Acute dystonic reaction
opts.rat: #4 — Unlikely: dystonia is an early (hours–days) sustained spasm, not chronic choreoathetoid movements.

opts.text: A primary neurologic movement disorder such as Huntington disease
opts.rat: #5 — LEAST likely given the clear long-term antipsychotic exposure, but worth excluding as a mimic.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED.

opts.text: Perform and document an AIMS to quantify severity and establish a baseline for tracking response
opts.rat: Standardized assessment is essential to diagnose, grade, and monitor tardive dyskinesia.

opts.text: Notify psychiatry to re-evaluate and minimize the antipsychotic dose or switch to a lower-TD-risk agent
opts.rat: Reducing dopamine-blockade exposure with a lower-risk agent is a core management step.

opts.text: Start an FDA-approved VMAT2 inhibitor (valbenazine or deutetrabenazine) with appropriate monitoring
opts.rat: VMAT2 inhibitors are first-line, evidence-based treatment for TD.

opts.text: Assess swallowing/aspiration risk and gait safety, and provide non-judgmental psychosocial support
opts.rat: Orofacial movements can impair swallowing and TD is stigmatizing; safety and support are part of care.

opts.text: Rule out mimics (metoclopramide use, withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia, primary movement disorders, dentures)
opts.rat: Excluding other causes confirms the diagnosis and avoids mismanagement.

opts.text: Administer benztropine or diphenhydramine to treat the movements
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — anticholinergics worsen TD; they are for acute dystonia/pseudoparkinsonism.

opts.text: Increase the haloperidol dose to suppress the movements
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — raising the antipsychotic only masks TD transiently and worsens the underlying disorder.

opts.text: Abruptly stop the antipsychotic today to remove the cause
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — abrupt withdrawal can cause withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia and psychotic relapse; changes are tapered by the prescriber.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Recognize the movements as possible TD and complete an AIMS, distinguishing it from dystonia/akathisia/pseudoparkinsonism and ruling out mimics
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Accurate recognition and a baseline AIMS drive correct, safe management.

opts.text: Notify psychiatry/the prescriber with the findings and AIMS score
opts.rat: #2 — Escalation enables medication decisions (dose change/switch, starting a VMAT2 inhibitor).

opts.text: Carry out the plan to minimize/switch the antipsychotic without abruptly stopping it and start the prescribed VMAT2 inhibitor
opts.rat: #3 — The definitive interventions, done safely to avoid relapse and withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia.

opts.text: Assess swallowing/aspiration and gait safety and provide education and non-judgmental support
opts.rat: #4 — Protects function and supports adherence and coping with a visible condition.

opts.text: Arrange ongoing AIMS monitoring and follow-up with metabolic/QTc surveillance
opts.rat: #5 — Ensures the response is tracked and the VMAT2 inhibitor/antipsychotic are monitored over time.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: Classify each finding as the patient IMPROVING, WORSENING, or UNCHANGED.

opts.text: Six weeks after the antipsychotic was minimized and valbenazine started, her orofacial movements are noticeably less frequent and she eats more comfortably
opts.rat: Reduced movements and better function indicate the VMAT2 inhibitor and dose optimization are working.

opts.text: At three months her AIMS score is substantially lower with no psychotic relapse on the optimized regimen
opts.rat: A falling AIMS score with maintained psychiatric stability is the goal of treatment.

opts.text: She tolerates the VMAT2 inhibitor without excess sedation, new parkinsonism, or QTc prolongation
opts.rat: Good tolerability supports continuing effective therapy.

opts.text: After benztropine was added for the movements, the orofacial dyskinesia is now worse
opts.rat: ⚠️ Anticholinergics worsen TD — stop the benztropine; it is not a treatment for tardive dyskinesia.

opts.text: After the antipsychotic was abruptly discontinued, the dyskinesia intensified and psychotic symptoms re-emerged
opts.rat: ⚠️ Abrupt withdrawal caused withdrawal-emergent dyskinesia and relapse — changes must be tapered by the prescriber.

opts.text: The tongue and mouth movements now interfere with chewing and she coughs while drinking
opts.rat: ⚠️ Swallowing is now impaired with aspiration risk — assess swallow/airway and involve speech/dietitian.

opts.text: Two weeks into the VMAT2 inhibitor her AIMS score is about the same as baseline
opts.rat: Benefit may take several weeks; continue therapy and reassess at the next interval.

opts.text: Her psychiatric symptoms remain stable and well controlled on the optimized regimen
opts.rat: Stable mental status is the desired baseline to maintain while treating the TD.

opts.text: At six months the movements are markedly improved and she is engaged in care with regular AIMS monitoring
opts.rat: Sustained reduction in movements with ongoing monitoring confirms effective, safe management.
